jokes.text: Michael was sharing his bed for the first time in years. Unfortunately, it was with his younger brother, Buster.
jokes.setup: Michael sharing his bed for the first time in years suggests romantic progress
jokes.punchline: It's with his adult brother, completely subverting expectations
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with economical setup. The narrator's deadpan delivery of 'Unfortunately' makes it even funnier. Classic AD structure.

jokes.text: I don't think us sleeping together is working out.
jokes.setup: Michael addressing his brother about their sleeping arrangement
jokes.punchline: Phrasing makes it sound like a romantic breakup
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective double entendre. The awkward phrasing creates comedy through uncomfortable implications.

jokes.text: Absolutely. It's preemptive, you know, happens before something else. So... if you can predict aggression and you wanna squash it... I don't know why you're not typing?
jokes.setup: Michael helping George Michael with ethics homework about preemptive strikes
jokes.punchline: Michael's casual endorsement of preemptive war while helping with ethics, oblivious to the irony
jokes.explanation: Character-specific comedy - Michael's business mindset applied to ethics creates ironic comedy that fits his personality perfectly.

jokes.text: Well, you just passed my ethics test.
jokes.setup: George Michael expresses concern about cheating on his ethics essay
jokes.punchline: Michael claims this moral concern is itself an ethics test, missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that shows Michael's ability to rationalize anything. The irony is perfectly character-driven.

jokes.text: Mmm. No ring.
jokes.setup: George Michael listening to his ethics teacher talk about brutal punishments
jokes.punchline: He's completely distracted by checking if she's married instead of the lesson content
jokes.explanation: Perfect teenage boy behavior - completely missing serious content while focused on romantic interest. Economical and relatable.

jokes.text: The S.E.C. was onto us. Your father thought it would make us look charitable. He must have forged my signature.
jokes.setup: Michael discovers his parents apparently adopted a child
jokes.punchline: It was a calculated PR move to avoid SEC charges, not genuine charity
jokes.explanation: Perfectly cynical Bluth family move - turning adoption into a business strategy. Dark humor that defines the family's moral compass.

jokes.text: Well, maybe I'll get a son who will... finish his cottage cheese!
jokes.setup: Lucille discussing the adopted child situation
jokes.punchline: Her petty complaint about Buster's eating habits as motivation for replacing him
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille - turning a major family decision into a petty criticism. The specificity of cottage cheese makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I don't have the milk of mother's kindness in me anymore. Yeah, that udder's been dry for a while now, hasn't it?
jokes.setup: Lucille uses a metaphor about maternal instincts
jokes.punchline: Michael extends the cow metaphor with 'udder,' making it more literal and crude
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay that takes Lucille's metaphor and makes it more absurd. Michael's willingness to insult his mother is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Besides, when was the last time you slept with someone? - Buster.
jokes.setup: Gob asking when Michael last slept with someone
jokes.punchline: Michael's honest answer references the opening bed-sharing situation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that recontextualizes the opening joke. Perfect timing and economy - the audience gets the reference immediately.

jokes.text: Lindsay believed that George Michael wanted to fix up his father... so he could fill the role of his mother.
jokes.setup: George Michael expressing interest in his teacher
jokes.punchline: Lindsay completely misinterprets it as wanting to set up his father
jokes.explanation: Classic AD miscommunication that drives plot. Lindsay's self-absorbed interpretation creates the misunderstanding that fuels the episode.

jokes.text: You're my aunt. - That doesn't matter. Aunts can fill that role.
jokes.setup: George Michael pointing out Lindsay is his aunt
jokes.punchline: Lindsay dismissing familial boundaries as irrelevant
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's inappropriate boundary-crossing is perfectly in character. The cringe comes from her casual dismissal of obvious problems.

jokes.text: Lindsay had never been more proud of anything she had said in her entire life.
jokes.setup: Lindsay giving inappropriate advice about maternal figures
jokes.punchline: Narrator noting this terrible advice as Lindsay's proudest moment
jokes.explanation: Devastating character insight delivered deadpan. The narrator's observation makes Lindsay's delusion even funnier.

jokes.text: Yikes.
jokes.setup: Lindsay's inappropriate suggestion about maternal roles
jokes.punchline: George Michael's understated horror at what he's just heard
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction - George Michael's mild 'yikes' perfectly captures the awkwardness without overplaying it.

jokes.text: That's a young, young, young, girl.
jokes.setup: Gob introducing his 18-year-old date Shannon
jokes.punchline: Michael's increasingly emphatic repetition of 'young' shows his discomfort
jokes.explanation: The escalating repetition shows Michael's growing horror. Simple but effective way to show discomfort.

jokes.text: Oh... 'Nagarma,' you've got a mustache. Hey! I mean, you've got milk on your mustache. Oh. I mean, you've got a milk mustache.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to be polite to Gob's date Nazhgalia
jokes.punchline: His increasingly desperate attempts to cover his mistake about her facial hair
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe comedy - Michael's panic creates escalating awkwardness. The physical comedy of digging himself deeper is relatable.

jokes.text: It's adorable. You look like one of those 'Got Milk' models.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to recover from the mustache comment
jokes.punchline: His attempt to be complimentary makes it worse by referencing advertising
jokes.explanation: Continues the cringe sequence but feels less sharp. The 'Got Milk' reference dates the joke and feels forced.

jokes.text: And I watch 'em over and over.
jokes.setup: Cindi expressing devotion to George Sr.'s self-help tapes
jokes.punchline: The obsessive repetition suggests unhealthy fixation
jokes.explanation: Sets up Cindi as an obsessive character. Simple but effective character establishment.

jokes.text: I've also lost four pounds on your low-carb Bluth Banana Jail Bars.
jokes.setup: Cindi discussing George Sr.'s self-help empire
jokes.punchline: The absurd product name combining prison life with diet trends
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd product name that perfectly captures George Sr.'s opportunistic commercialization of his prison experience.

jokes.text: That's a good place to start.
jokes.setup: Cindi saying she wants to learn 'at your feet'
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s suggestive response to what should be about spiritual learning
jokes.explanation: Shows George Sr.'s sleazy nature perfectly. The innuendo is obvious but fits his character's opportunism.

jokes.text: Hey, Linds, is it my imagination... or does this rape room have the same floor plan as our kitchen?
jokes.setup: Michael and Lindsay at parent-teacher night
jokes.punchline: Casual observation that a concerning classroom resembles their home
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny and absurd - the casual delivery of 'rape room' combined with architectural observation creates perfect AD dark humor.

jokes.text: Did you meet Ms. Baerly yet, the ethics teacher? 'Cause I just did. No, not yet. Look, these are our cabinets.
jokes.setup: Lindsay trying to discuss the teacher while Michael notices familiar architecture
jokes.punchline: Their competing obsessions create cross-talk
jokes.explanation: Continues the model home visual gag while showing how the siblings talk past each other. Layered comedy typical of AD.

jokes.text: You'd think that would turn him off to the entire concept.
jokes.setup: Discussion about George Michael wanting a mother figure
jokes.punchline: Michael suggesting their mother and Lindsay should put kids off motherhood entirely
jokes.explanation: Sharp family roast that's both mean and accurate. Michael's willingness to insult family members is character-consistent.

jokes.text: Mmm. No ring.
jokes.setup: Michael seeing Ms. Baerly for the first time
jokes.punchline: Same reaction as George Michael earlier, showing they're more alike than expected
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that shows father and son are more similar than they realize. Sets up the romantic competition.

jokes.text: I guess that's the 'Rrrr' of it. You know, so frustrating.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to discuss ethics with Ms. Baerly
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical pirate-like sound while trying to sound philosophical
jokes.explanation: Shows Michael floundering while trying to impress. The random 'Rrrr' sound is awkward in a funny way.

jokes.text: Yeah. But she's dead.
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly asking if Michael has a wife
jokes.punchline: Michael's matter-of-fact delivery of tragic information
jokes.explanation: Michael's casual delivery of heavy information creates dark comedy. The unexpected response subverts normal dating conversation.

jokes.text: God, why are we laughing? I don't... Well, it's the Mr. Daniels stroke thing... all over again.
jokes.setup: Both laughing inappropriately about Michael's dead wife
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging their pattern of laughing at tragic news
jokes.explanation: Self-aware comedy about their inappropriate responses. Characters recognizing their own awkwardness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You've got to strip them down to next to nothing before you can even tell.
jokes.setup: Lucille discussing the adopted Korean child's gender
jokes.punchline: Phrasing that sounds inappropriately sexual when discussing a child
jokes.explanation: Lucille's inappropriate phrasing creates uncomfortable humor. The innuendo is unintentional but fits her character's obliviousness.

jokes.text: This is 'Annyong,' who your father and I have adopted.
jokes.setup: Introducing the new family member
jokes.punchline: The casual way she presents this major family change
jokes.explanation: Lucille's matter-of-fact delivery of shocking news is perfectly in character. The understated presentation makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Lucille could see that her son was concerned, even jealous. And she knew how it felt to be overlooked.
jokes.setup: Michael's reaction to the adoption news
jokes.punchline: Lucille's rare moment of empathy immediately weaponized against her son
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character insight - Lucille's rare empathy immediately twisted into manipulation. The narrator's dry delivery makes her psychology clear.

jokes.text: Yes. Annyong is your brother now.
jokes.setup: Michael's obvious discomfort with the adoption
jokes.punchline: Lucille's deliberate emphasis to maximize Michael's discomfort
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille manipulation - using the adoption to hurt Michael emotionally. Her timing and emphasis show calculated cruelty.

jokes.text: No, no. That was my Mom. She just had... a 'little Korean' dropped off. - Ooh, that sounds good. Let's have that.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his phone call about Annyong
jokes.punchline: Ms. Baerly thinking he's describing a restaurant dish
jokes.explanation: Brilliant miscommunication joke. The phrasing perfectly sets up the food misunderstanding, and both characters' responses are logical.

jokes.text: This may not have been my best idea.
jokes.setup: Michael and Ms. Baerly attempting to sneak into the model home
jokes.punchline: Michael's massive understatement as their plan clearly goes wrong
jokes.explanation: Classic understatement humor. Michael's calm assessment amid obvious chaos is funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: You took something I liked. I took something you liked.
jokes.setup: Gob confronting Michael about Marta
jokes.punchline: Gob's childish tit-for-tat mentality applied to relationships
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob logic - reducing complex relationships to simple transactions. His petty mindset is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: 'Nashbagerlajam.'
jokes.setup: Michael trying to remember Nazhgalia's name
jokes.punchline: Completely mangling the pronunciation into gibberish
jokes.explanation: Continues the running gag of mispronouncing her name. The specific mangling is absurd and memorable.

jokes.text: Who's 'N. Bahn-Ahden?'
jokes.setup: Gob claiming the relationship was casual
jokes.punchline: Nazhgalia revealing she's already monogrammed items, showing serious commitment
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - her attempt at their combined names shows she's way more invested than Gob realized. The specific name combo is absurd.

jokes.text: You mean, you love her like... like she loves Saddam. - Right? - No, no. I mean, like, love her love her.
jokes.setup: George Michael expressing romantic feelings for his teacher
jokes.punchline: Michael desperately trying to reframe it as academic interest
jokes.explanation: Great father-son awkwardness. Michael's attempt to deflect creates more confusion. The Saddam comparison is perfectly uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Your Uncle Gob slept with her.
jokes.setup: Michael realizing he needs to tell George Michael the truth
jokes.punchline: Michael telling a different, less painful lie instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Michael choosing a different lie rather than the truth. Perfect parenting failure that's both protective and dishonest.

jokes.text: He's not that exact of a target shooter, and he just kind of sprays it everywhere.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Gob would target the wrong person for revenge
jokes.punchline: Military metaphor applied to Gob's romantic incompetence
jokes.explanation: Exceptional metaphor that perfectly captures Gob's character. The military language makes it funnier and more memorable.

jokes.text: Like your cousin. - That's a bad example, but...
jokes.setup: Michael suggesting George Michael take someone his own age to the dance
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately realizing his suggestion of cousin-dating is inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Self-aware reference to the show's ongoing George Michael-Maeby subplot. Michael's immediate backtrack shows awareness of the family dysfunction.

jokes.text: I defaced a rare book to get that picture of Saddam Hussein in a bathing suit.
jokes.setup: George Michael's elaborate gesture for his teacher
jokes.punchline: The absurd lengths he went to for an inappropriate gift
jokes.explanation: Perfect teenage logic - committing a crime for a hopeless romantic gesture. The specificity of 'bathing suit' makes it absurdly inappropriate.

jokes.text: Okay, that is our exact outdoor fire pit.
jokes.setup: Continuing observation about identical layouts
jokes.punchline: Even outdoor features are copied from their home
jokes.explanation: Continues running gag about model home similarities

jokes.text: Yes, Annyong. Your name's Annyong. We all know you're Annyong. Annyong. Annyong. Annyong.
jokes.setup: Annyong saying hello in Korean
jokes.punchline: George Michael's frustrated repetition, not realizing 'annyong' means 'hello'
jokes.explanation: Establishes the classic Annyong running gag. George Michael's frustration with what he thinks is repetitive name-saying is perfect miscommunication.

jokes.text: Oh, great, another uncle to compete with.
jokes.setup: George Michael learning Maeby might go to the dance with Annyong
jokes.punchline: His jealousy toward his new 'uncle' in the complicated family dynamic
jokes.explanation: Perfect reference to the show's complicated family tree and George Michael's ongoing romantic competition. The 'uncle' reference is layered.

jokes.text: Don't worry. George Michael's not in there. Those are the dumb kids.
jokes.setup: Michael visiting Ms. Baerly's classroom
jokes.punchline: Teacher casually dismissing her current students as 'dumb kids'
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny teacher behavior - casual dismissal of students she's supposed to help. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You really mean it? This isn't one of 'my wife died' jokes?
jokes.setup: Michael saying he can't continue seeing her
jokes.punchline: Ms. Baerly referencing their pattern of inappropriate laughter
jokes.explanation: Self-aware callback to their earlier inappropriate responses. Shows the characters recognize their own dysfunction.

jokes.text: It was a preemptive strike. My brother would've tried to sleep with you.
jokes.setup: Ms. Baerly calling Michael's lie unethical
jokes.punchline: Michael using the preemptive strike concept from the opening homework scene
jokes.explanation: Brilliant full-circle callback to the opening scene. Michael applying his own ethics lesson to justify his lie is perfect character consistency and irony.

jokes.text: He's pinching the camera. He's on to us. He's found the camera. Get out of there. He's got the camera. Abort! Abort!
jokes.setup: Police trying to secretly record George Sr.
jokes.punchline: Their cover blown in the most obvious way possible
jokes.explanation: Standard escalating panic comedy. Functional but not particularly original or character-specific.

jokes.text: Get the dog. Clean the dog. We are cleaning the dog!
jokes.setup: Police team panicking about blown surveillance
jokes.punchline: Desperate improvisation with nonsensical dog-cleaning cover story
jokes.explanation: Absurd panic response that's funny in its randomness. The specificity of 'cleaning the dog' as a cover story is amusing.

jokes.text: You're trying to steal from the wrong man. Watch your back, my little immigrant friend!
jokes.setup: Lucille dropping off Annyong
jokes.punchline: Her immediate paranoia and hostility toward the child she adopted
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille paranoia - seeing threats from a child. Her dramatic delivery and immediate suspicion are perfectly in character.

jokes.text: So you're not letting him in because he doesn't share your perfectly shaped nose... your round eye-shaped eyes, your strong square jaw. - Thanks. You wanna dance?
jokes.setup: Lindsay defending Annyong against perceived discrimination
jokes.punchline: Steve Holt taking her descriptions as compliments and asking her to dance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - Lindsay's anti-racism speech accidentally becomes flirtation. Steve Holt's oblivious response is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: I slept with the ethics teacher. I'm sorry.
jokes.setup: Gob trying to do the right thing
jokes.punchline: His confession is actually making things worse
jokes.explanation: Gob's attempt at nobility backfires spectacularly. His misguided confession creates more problems, which is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I slept with the ethics teacher. I'm sorry. - What are you talking about? - Lindsay told me that you liked her and I'm not proud of this... but... l... Mrs. Whitehead.
jokes.setup: Gob confessing to sleeping with the ethics teacher
jokes.punchline: He slept with the wrong teacher entirely
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - Gob's grand gesture is completely pointless because he targeted the wrong person.

jokes.text: In fact, Gob mistook the civics teacher... for the ethics teacher Michael was dating.
jokes.setup: Gob's dramatic confession
jokes.punchline: Revelation that he confessed to the wrong transgression entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect AD misdirection - Gob's confession is meaningless because he got the wrong teacher. The complexity of the misunderstanding is brilliantly constructed.

jokes.text: Hey, uh, you wanna dance? - Sure, why not?
jokes.setup: George Michael finally asking someone to dance
jokes.punchline: Getting exactly what he originally wanted with his cousin
jokes.explanation: Sweet callback to his earlier plan, now executed with his cousin. The irony of getting what he wanted in the 'wrong' way is perfectly AD.

jokes.text: I can't believe that would have worked.
jokes.setup: George Michael successfully asking Maeby to dance
jokes.punchline: His surprise that his simple, direct approach actually worked
jokes.explanation: Perfect teenage self-doubt - George Michael's amazement at basic social success is endearingly realistic.

jokes.text: That's exactly what I'll do.
jokes.setup: Buster seeing Annyong with candles
jokes.punchline: Buster's immediate competitive response to light his own candle
jokes.explanation: Classic Buster competitiveness with children. His immediate mimicry of Annyong is perfectly in character for his arrested development.
